chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: Mindful Eating: A Path to a Healthy Body

questions.question: What is the meaning of the Sanskrit saying 'annena jtni jivanti' mentioned in the chapter?
questions.answer: Food gives life to living beings.
questions.solution: This saying emphasizes the essential nature of food for the survival of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which nutrient is the primary source of 'instant energy' for a marathon runner?
questions.answer: Glucose (a type of carbohydrate).
questions.solution: As per the text, glucose provides immediate energy required during or after physically demanding activities.

questions.question: Classify the following as Energy-giving, Body-building, or Protective food: (i) Rice, (ii) Milk, (iii) Lemon.
questions.answer: (i) Energy-giving, (ii) Body-building, (iii) Protective.
questions.solution: Rice is rich in carbohydrates (energy), milk is rich in proteins (growth/repair), and lemon is rich in Vitamin C (protection).

questions.question: True or False: Roughage provides essential nutrients to our body.
questions.answer: False.
questions.solution: Roughage (dietary fibre) does not provide nutrients but helps in the smooth passage of stools and removing undigested food.

questions.question: Which mineral deficiency causes swelling at the front of the neck (Goitre)?
questions.answer: Iodine.
questions.solution: Symptoms of neck swelling are linked to a deficiency of iodine in the diet, as seen in the Himalayan region case study.

questions.question: Why is it recommended to wash vegetables before cutting or peeling them rather than after?
questions.answer: To prevent the loss of water-soluble vitamins like Vitamin C.
questions.solution: Washing already cut or peeled vegetables can wash away essential vitamins and minerals that are exposed.

questions.question: Explain the role of fat in the survival of a polar bear during hibernation.
questions.answer: Fat stored under the skin acts as an energy source during the months-long winter sleep.
questions.solution: Polar bears accumulate thick layers of fat which sustain them without eating for months by providing stored energy.

questions.question: A student adds iodine solution to a slice of potato and it turns blue-black. What does this indicate?
questions.answer: The presence of starch (a carbohydrate).
questions.solution: The iodine test is a standard method to detect the presence of starch in food items.

questions.question: Compare the nutritional value of potato wafers and roasted chana. Which one is better and why?
questions.answer: Roasted chana is better because it has more proteins, fibre, and lower fats compared to wafers.
questions.solution: Based on Activity 3.9, chana has 18.64g protein and 16.8g fibre, while wafers have only 7g protein and 4.8g fibre.

questions.question: What are 'nutri-cereals'? Give two examples native to India.
questions.answer: Highly nutritious grains called millets; Examples: Jowar, Bajra, Ragi, or Sanwa.
questions.solution: These crops are integral to the Indian diet and are rich in vitamins, minerals like iron and calcium, and dietary fibres.

questions.question: Define 'Food Miles' and state one reason why reducing them is beneficial.
questions.answer: The distance food travels from producer to consumer; benefit: reduces transport pollution and supports local farmers.
questions.solution: Lowering food miles ensures fresher food and minimizes the carbon footprint caused by long-distance transportation.

questions.question: A person suffers from bleeding and swollen gums. Identify the deficiency disease and suggest the best immediate dietary fix from the textbook.
questions.answer: Scurvy (Vitamin C deficiency); Best fix: include citrus fruits like lemons and oranges.
questions.solution: Bleeding gums are a hallmark symptom of scurvy, which was historically cured by sailors using citrus fruits (James Lind's observation).

questions.question: How does the 'PM POSHAN' (Mid Day Meal) initiative improve the health and nutrition of school children?
questions.answer: By providing balanced food containing required proteins, energy, and other nutrients.
questions.solution: Initiated based on Coluthur Gopalan's research, it addresses widespread nutritional deficiencies in the Indian student population.

questions.question: A solution turns violet when treated with copper sulphate and caustic soda. What nutrient is present?
questions.answer: Protein.
questions.solution: The combination of these two chemicals reacting to form a violet colour is the standard laboratory test for proteins.

questions.question: Analyse the statement: 'Health is the Ultimate Wealth.' How does consuming junk food regularly contradict this?
questions.answer: Junk foods cause obesity and provide high calories without essential nutrients like vitamins and minerals.
questions.solution: Frequent consumption of junk food like wafers and carbonated drinks leads to health problems, negating the wealth of a healthy body.

questions.question: Compare the 'chulha' and 'sil-batta' with their modern kitchen equivalents. Why have these practices changed?
questions.answer: Modern equivalents: Gas stove and electrical grinder; changed due to technological development and ease of use.
questions.solution: Technological improvements provide faster, cleaner, and less manually intensive ways to prepare food.

questions.question: What is the function of water in our diet besides quenching thirst?
questions.answer: It helps absorb nutrients and remove waste from the body through sweat and urine.
questions.solution: Water is a vital solvent for biological processes and temperature regulation through waste expulsion.

questions.question: A twelve-year-old child's diet chart is being prepared. Why should it include millets and fortified salt?
questions.answer: Millets provide minerals/fibre and fortified salt prevents iodine deficiency diseases like goitre.
questions.solution: Growing children require diverse minerals and vitamins; fortification ensures even common items like salt provide protective nutrients.

questions.question: Which vitamin prevents the disease 'Rickets' (weak/bent bones)?
questions.answer: Vitamin D.
questions.solution: Vitamin D is essential for calcium absorption into bones, preventing rickets especially in growing children.

questions.question: Explain how food miles are related to environmental sustainability.
questions.answer: Lower food miles reduce the pollution cost of transportation and support local biodiversity.
questions.solution: When we eat local, seasonal food, we minimize the energy used for shipping and promote the health of our local planet/environment.

questions.question: What is 'fortification' of food? Give one example from the chapter.
questions.answer: Adding extra nutrients to food during processing; Example: Iodised salt or fortified baby foods.
questions.solution: Government initiatives and agencies like FSSAI encourage fortification to address common nutritional gaps in the population.

questions.question: If Mishti eats only white bread and biscuits, why might she suffer from constipation?
questions.answer: Because these highly processed foods lack dietary fibre (roughage) needed for smooth bowel movements.
questions.solution: As per Case study of Medu/Mishti, a lack of roughage from wholegrains and vegetables leads to stomach ache and constipation.

questions.question: Name two plant sources and two animal sources of protein.
questions.answer: Plant: Pulses, Peas; Animal: Egg, Milk.
questions.solution: The textbook classifies protein sources into these two broad categories based on their origin.

questions.question: Which component of food do Sportspersons need in larger quantities and why?
questions.answer: Proteins; for growth and building/repair of muscles.
questions.solution: High physical activity requires significant body-building nutrients to sustain muscle development.

questions.question: Describe the 'Paper Patch Test' and state which nutrient it detects.
questions.answer: Rubbing food on paper to see if it leaves a persistent oily patch; it detects Fats.
questions.solution: If the paper remains translucent/oily after drying, it indicates the presence of oils or fats in the food sample.

questions.question: What does FSSAI stand for?
questions.answer: Food Safety and Standards Authority of India.
questions.solution: FSSAI is the government agency responsible for regulating food quality and safety standards nationwide.

questions.question: Why are vitamins and minerals called 'protective nutrients'?
questions.answer: Because they protect our body from various diseases and keep us healthy.
questions.solution: Unlike energy-giving or body-building foods, vitamins/minerals are primarily responsible for maintaining health and immunity.

questions.question: Link the traditional food items of Karnataka to its locally grown crops based on the provided data.
questions.answer: Karnataka grows rice, ragi, and coconut; traditional items include Idli, Dosa, and Ragi mudde.
questions.solution: Dietary habits are historically rooted in the crops that can be successfully cultivated in the local soil and climate.
